summary:
Market Analysis Update: March 25, 2026

The Canadian bond market is navigating a period of aggressive "bear flattening" as yields surge following the March 24th yield shock in the US Treasury market. Over the last 30 days, Government of Canada 2-year yields have climbed from 2.54% to 2.95%, while 5-year yields have jumped from 2.90% to 3.14%, after peaking at 3.20%. This rapid ascent is primarily driven by the "weak 2-year auction" in the US, which has spilled over into Canadian markets, forcing a repricing of risk despite the Bank of Canada holding its policy rate steady at 2.25%. The 2-5 year spread has collapsed from 36 basis points in early February to a narrow 19 basis points today, signaling that the market is bracing for a "higher-for-longer" interest rate environment as inflation concerns override previous expectations of monetary easing.

For our institution, this environment demands immediate recalibration of both asset and liability pricing to protect our Net Interest Margin (NIM). The elevated 5-year yield acts as the benchmark for our fixed-rate mortgages; to avoid margin erosion, we must implement a corresponding hike in retail mortgage rates to reflect the increased cost of wholesale funding. Simultaneously, the spike in 2-year yields—now 70 basis points above the BoC policy rate—is driving up the cost of term deposits. We must raise GIC rates to remain competitive and maintain liquidity, yet the narrowing spread between our cost of funds (driven by the 2Y) and our lending benchmarks (driven by the 5Y) creates a significant "margin squeeze." Close monitoring of the US-Canada yield correlation is vital, as any further volatility in US Treasuries will continue to pull Canadian yields higher, regardless of domestic economic indicators.
